I think Skyblock, at its core, is the ultimate challenge in resource management. You spawn on a tiny island, in an empty universe. All you have is a tree, some supplies, and some dirt to stand on.  
You have to treasure every dirt block because if one falls into the void, there's no way to replace it. And as you carefully navigate your absurd circumstance, you gain a new appreciation for the few things you have, as you meticulously use them to their fullest extent. With nothing but some ice, lava and saplings, you slowly transform this empty expanse into a world of your very own. Skyblock teaches us that no matter how ridiculous the odds may seem, within us resides the power to overcome these challenges and achieve something beautiful. And one day, we'll look back at where we started, and be amazed by how far we've come.

Hey dude, 
I noticed your clicking is very loud in this video, so i thought id let you know this trick thing. (Done in Audacity)
- When recording mic, leave 5 seconds before talking where you just type random keys and click a bunch of time (It will sample what the clicking sounds like)
- When editing the audio, select  (highlight) the 5 seconds where you just clicked on the audio timeline
- Go up to effect and click Noise removal
- Then click "Get Noise Profile"
- Unselect the highlighted area
- Go back up to Effect and click noise removal (with nothing highlighted)
- Click Ok

This should make the click sound minimal. Hope this helped.

Time for some maths and I hope Techno might see this. How effective is Protection V on each piece?
Calculating it involves a complex series of equations but should be simple enough to follow through here. Firstly, an EPF (Enchantment Protection Factor) is calculated then multiplied by the amount of damage already going through. It will make sense later on.

Going into the basics, the formula for finding Protection's EPF is ( 6 + level ^2) x 0.75 / 3
Since the level is 5, it is subbed into the equation like:
EPF = (6 + 25) x 0.75 / 3
EPF = 7.75 This value is rounded down so... the EPF of a Protection V is 7.

In the next formula, we take that EPF, multiply it by a random value from 0.5 to 1, then multiply that value by 4.
So since our EPF is 7, we multiply by anything from 0.5 to 1 to get any value from 3.5 to 7. Then when this is multiplied by 4, the possible range of values comes to 14 to 28.
Now, the current amount of damage going through is taken into account. As a full set of Leather Armor reduces 28% of damage, 72% of damage goes through. Therefore, this 0.72 is multiplied by anything from 14 to 28. Below are the minimum and maximum values it can add to current damage reduction.

14 x 0.72 = 10.08%
28 x 0.72 = 20.16%

So now these just get added to the normal damage reduction of a Leather Tunic and Pants.
Leather Tunic reduces 12% of damage so Protection V Leather Tunic reduces 22.08% to 32.16% (27.12% average) of damage.
Leather Pants reduce 8% of damage so Protection V Leather Pants reduce 18.08% to 28.16% (23.12% average) of damage.

Some comparisons:
An Iron Chestplate reduces 24% of damage and a Diamond Chestplate reduces 32% of damage.
Chainmail Leggings reduce 16% of damage, Iron Leggings reduce 20% of damage and Diamond Leggings reduce 24% of damage.

There is a lot of chance involved but as you can see, Armorer has the potential to outperform both Creepertamer and Tim at the same time! However, it also has the potential to reduce less damage than even Iron Armor. But on average, it is better than an Iron Chestplate and close to Diamond Leggings strength. Sometimes that extra potential is needed where a force exists that is just too strong to face with conventional Diamond Armor.

So it's up to you to believe me and test it out yourself but while Armorer may appear weak, it can one of the strongest kits in Blitz.
